title: The __init__ Constructor
summary: Learn how __init__ automatically sets up a new object the moment it is created, and why that makes your classes so much more powerful.

blocks.md:
Imagine ordering a coffee. The moment you place the order, the barista already knows: what size, what flavour, your name for the cup. You did not have to hand those details over one by one after the cup appeared — they came built-in from the start.

That is exactly what `__init__` does for Python objects. It is a special method that runs **automatically** the instant you create a new instance of a class. By the time you have a shiny new object in your hands, all its starting details are already set.

blocks.text: Classes vs Instances — a quick reminder

blocks.title: Blueprint vs Building
blocks.md: A **class** is like an architect's blueprint. It describes what every building of that type will look like. An **instance** is one actual building constructed from that blueprint. You can build many buildings from one blueprint, and each building can have its own address, colour, and number of floors.

blocks.code:
class Dog:
    pass

rex = Dog()   # rex is one instance
bella = Dog() # bella is a different instance

print(rex)   # different objects in memory
print(bella)
blocks.caption: Two instances from the same class — both are Dogs, but they live at different places in memory.

blocks.md: Right now `Dog` is empty. Every dog is identical and anonymous. Let's fix that with `__init__`.

blocks.code:
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        self.name = name
        self.breed = breed

rex = Dog("Rex", "Labrador")
print(rex.name)
print(rex.breed)
blocks.caption: Python calls __init__ automatically. You pass the arguments after the class name.

blocks.md:
Let's break down what happened line by line:

- `def __init__(self, name, breed):` — defines the setup method. It always receives `self` as its first argument.
- `self.name = name` — stores the value of `name` **on the object itself**. Think of it as pinning a name tag to that specific dog.
- `Dog("Rex", "Labrador")` — Python creates a new `Dog` and immediately calls `__init__` with `"Rex"` and `"Labrador"`. You never see that call — Python makes it for you.

blocks.title: Self = 'this very object'
blocks.md: `self` is just a name (you could technically use any word, but **never do** — always use `self`). It refers to the specific instance being set up right now. When Python runs `rex = Dog("Rex", "Labrador")`, it passes `rex` as `self` automatically. So `self.name = name` really means `rex.name = "Rex"`.

blocks.text: Each Instance Has Its Own Attributes

blocks.code:
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        self.name = name
        self.breed = breed

rex = Dog("Rex", "Labrador")
bella = Dog("Bella", "Poodle")

print(rex.name, rex.breed)
print(bella.name, bella.breed)
blocks.caption: rex and bella are separate objects. Changing one does not affect the other.

blocks.md: Sometimes you want a sensible default so callers do not have to provide every value. You can give `__init__` parameters default values just like any other Python function.

blocks.code:
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, breed="Unknown"):
        self.name = name
        self.breed = breed

dog1 = Dog("Max", "Beagle")  # breed provided
dog2 = Dog("Buddy")          # breed uses default

print(dog1.name, dog1.breed)
print(dog2.name, dog2.breed)
blocks.caption: If you skip 'breed', Python uses the default value 'Unknown'.

blocks.title: Put defaults at the end
blocks.md: Parameters with defaults must always come **after** parameters without defaults. `def __init__(self, name, breed="Unknown")` is fine. `def __init__(self, breed="Unknown", name)` would raise a `SyntaxError`.

blocks.title: Don't call __init__ yourself
blocks.md: You should **never** write `rex.__init__("Rex", "Labrador")` in normal code. Python calls it for you when you write `Dog("Rex", "Labrador")`. Calling it manually would run the setup code a second time on an already-created object, which is almost always a bug.

blocks.caption: __init__ sets up owner and balance; deposit uses self to access them later.

blocks.md: Notice that `deposit` can read `self.owner` and `self.balance` because `__init__` stored them on the object. Every method in the class can access attributes set in `__init__` through `self`.
Not every attribute has to come directly from a parameter. You can compute values inside `__init__` too.

blocks.code:
class Rectangle:
    def __init__(self, width, height):
        self.width = width
        self.height = height
        self.area = width * height  # computed at creation time

r = Rectangle(5, 3)
print(r.area)
blocks.caption: self.area is calculated once during __init__ and stored on the object.

blocks.question: What does Python do when you write `dog = Dog("Rex", "Labrador")`?
options: It creates a new Dog object and you must call __init__ separately to set it up.
It creates a new Dog object and automatically calls __init__(self, "Rex", "Labrador") for you.
It calls __init__ but you still need to assign self.name and self.breed manually afterwards.
It raises an error because __init__ is a private method.
blocks.explanation: Python creates the new instance and immediately calls __init__ with the arguments you provided. You never need to call __init__ yourself — it is automatic.

keyTakeaways: `__init__` runs automatically every time you create a new instance — you never call it yourself.
Use `self.attribute = value` inside `__init__` to store data on the specific object being created.
Arguments you pass at `Dog("Rex")` flow directly into `__init__`'s parameters (after `self`).
Default parameter values let callers skip optional arguments: `def __init__(self, name, breed="Unknown")`.
A class is the blueprint; an instance is a real object built from it — each instance has its own independent attributes.

exercise.prompt: Create a class called `Movie` with an `__init__` method that accepts `title`, `director`, and `year`. Give `year` a default value of 2024. Then create two Movie instances: one with all three arguments, and one using only `title` and `director`. Print the `title` and `year` of each.
exercise.hint: Inside __init__, write self.title = title, self.director = director, and self.year = year. Then create instances like Movie("Inception", "Nolan", 2010) and Movie("Untitled", "Smith").
exercise.solution:
class Movie:
    def __init__(self, title, director, year=2024):
        self.title = title
        self.director = director
        self.year = year

m1 = Movie("Inception", "Christopher Nolan", 2010)
m2 = Movie("Untitled", "Jordan Smith")

print(m1.title, m1.year)
print(m2.title, m2.year)
